text: This journal switched with re-sleeve and lab cholecystectomy for a patient who had a sleeve gastrectomy in the past and then had put on weight. She initially was weighing about twenty one stones, came down to twelve stones and then got up to almost seventeen stones therefore I did the re-sleeve as you could see that the fundus was quite big then she had gold stones therefore I had to remove the gold bladder also the main purpose here is to demonstrate this duodenal switch and here you have the duodenum about four centimeters from the pylorus and urinal junction, dissected blunt and sharp using the thunder bait or harmonic or ligature or the sonization. dissected it after that lifted it up with a nylon tape and then a tan staple is used to divide it then the alum is measured from the aloesicle junction for about hundred centimeters sometimes hundred twenty five centimeters depending upon the patient's ability to understand and This is suspended from the anterior abdominal wall so that it is easy to reach this area easily.

The surgeon opens the duodenum near the cartilage and measures the ileum for another 150 centimeters from the suspended area. A small incision, approximately two centimeters, is made in the ileum, while a larger opening is created on the duodenum side. The surgeon uses a V-Loc PDS suture to perform a posterior two-layer suture followed by an anterior layer suture, ensuring the anastomosis is suspended without tension.

The surgeon performs a V-lock suture beyond the entrotomy, starting from the distal end and proceeding to the posterior layer for a through-and-through suture. This technique creates a watertight seal and eliminates shelf-like formations. The suture is continued anteriorly as a single layer, with simple sutures at the corners instead of kernel sutures.

The surgeon performs submucosal suturing, ensuring the edges are approximated to resemble a pencil line. An extra suture is placed at the corner to prevent gaps. At the end of the procedure, an orogastric tube is passed to check for patency.

The surgeon continues the first layer of anterior sutures, then creates enterotomies in both the biliopancreatic limb (BPL) and the alimentary limb at the suspended area. The anastomosis is completed, and the mesenteric defect at the ileoileal anastomosis is closed using a Prolene suture. The biliopancreatic limb is divided after creating a window in the mesentery. The operation concludes with the removal of excess blood and placement of a drain.
